speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how it started, with this-- with this placard, which sort of told us, in so many words, that they're going to establish a ghetto for certain people. And then a week later, they told the whole Nowogrodek population-- not just the Jewish-- not to come out for three days, to stay in their homes. And we knew. And also, the-- some local people saw that the ditches were already dug, and all the line that was prepared. And we already heard that something happened in another state. And usually, our execution followed the ones from Slonim. There was a-- it was a place called Slonim, another-- another town. Their executions came always shortly before us. So we knew that something in Slonim that would follow it, and everything. And the SS commanders were there. Plus the-- some volunteer army from Lithuania and Latvia and the Baltic states, Ukrainians, Belarussians, the volunteers. But they are the worst. You could see them, you know, once they're in town, something's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told everybody to stay home. And I remember it was Friday. We stayed, like, Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, for three days. Now the whole town must stay at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in December, beginning of December. To be exact, about, I think, about the seventh, or-- the sixth and the seventh, or the seventh and the eighth. Between those two days. Now I cannot exactly pinpoint exactly the date. I think it was the seventh. But the seventh, they took us there. But the actual execution were performed on the eighth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Friday night, it was a white night. The snow and the whole night were a real white night, you know? You could see. Even if you wanted to run away, you couldn't. And where to? There was nowhere to run. Everything was covered in snow and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this night, we knew that the next day, this-- this could be my last night. And you can imagine what-- what went through my mind. Nobody slept a second through the night. I went through a terrible, terrible sort of-- you can call it, you know, that-- the fear of death.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why? And how come? You know? Impossible, but it's happening. And I used to get hot, and I used to get cold and covered in-- in sweat. And I really did have the angst, that angst, you know, that somebody survived, who is waiting for his death in a cell to be executed. Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sisters. With both my sisters and a couple, the old couple who lived in this farm. They were relatives of my brother-in-law. Because I didn't have any parents. There were three-- my brother-in-law had two brothers. Their parents died very young, and they left them this farm. And the boys went out and learned trade, and they went to live in towns, and in other towns. And they left this relative couple to take care of the farm. But at this time, we were all-- we all came to live there, because there was food there, there was shelter there. There was everything. We-- we prepared for the winter. We dug out the potatoes with the garden, the vegetables. We put in sauerkraut to make sour-- pickled cucumbers. We had all sort of-- everything was prepared for the winter. We could stay for two years, on this food. And all sort of cereals. All sort of butter, special melted to last with honey. Everything, because it was a farm. We were very well prepared for the winter, the bread and everything. But we had to leave it all. And then they came in the morning. They came in the morning, the Germans. They said, for us-- because there was no men. There were two old people and three women. And women didn't count. Because they said in this [GERMAN], on this placard, they didn't mention anything about single women. They just said men with their wives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, you stay home here, and went to the other place, where there were young able women. [GERMAN], you come with us. So we decided we're not going to stay here. We're going-- we're going there, where the able, young, useful people go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were taken to a place which was previously a courthouse, the district court. They had three buildings and courtyards there. So they took us all. So we went there. We didn't go to the-- we didn't wait home to-- to be taken back. Because we knew if we ever have the smallest, tiniest chance, we have with the-- with the young and the able, not with the old and the sick and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went there. The people who stayed home were taken to a place where I used to go to school-- there was a convent there-- and taken there. And the young were taken to the courthouse. And we ended up in the courthouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were three buildings. It was a huge building. And there was a smaller building, and another smaller building. And they took us all in. And this was incredible cold day. I can still remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people took possessions with them and food, and we didn't take anything. We knew we-- we were going to die. So I figure, how could you eat? You'd be surprised. We were standing there. Everybody's freezing. Everybody's herded like a-- like in a cattle yard. We don't know what's-- what's going to happen the next minute. And people start eating. Incredible. I can never forget this. I said, how can we eat? In the face of this, what's going to happen to us, we are going to get killed. And people are eating. And people took possessions with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in a minute came the-- a few jeeps with armed-- this [GERMAN] group which had those patterned bombs and skulls on them. And they looked like with those long things, with those long fur coats. And they got out. They look like, you know, the executioners. And they were. So they said-- they said everybody disperse in three buildings and stay there. Don't move until allowed to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went into-- we were taken into this big building. And other people went into the smaller buildings. And herded into one room, we couldn't move. And we had to stay overnight. So one lady said, to the German guard, she said, it's not enough room. She said, we can't stretch. So he said, tomorrow will be enough room. We give you more room. And that's how they talked. And the next morning, of course, the three sisters, we said, we're not moving without each other. We will stick together, whatever happens. We're going and looking, and to my mind, how can-- can you escape from here? What can you do? It can't be! It can't be! I'm going to-- today I'm going to get killed. It can't be. And of course, the night was terrible. I was-- I had that angst, you know. I just went hot and cold and sweaty and shaking. And I know what it's-- what it's like to wait for you-- for the execution. And I will just think, why? I'm 18 years old, you know, and to live, you know? How could the world exist without me? [LAUGHS] I wanted out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the most important thing is I want to live to see the Germans defeated. That was the most important thing, motivation. So they've got to be defeated, and I want to see it. I want to live to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how are my parents going to take it, if their children, you know, got killed? I didn't know what's going on there. We thought it was only us, out of this district. Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] first thing, the moment they allowed us out, the three of us were out in the courtyard. We look, what can we do? My sister, being a dressmaker-- very classy one-- she had very good connections. And one of her clients was in that smaller building across-- across the court. This was where we were sitting, this was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, maybe they know something. Anyway, it's nice to be sort of with people who-- because we were lost. So we went into there, and my sister found her friends there. And they didn't say anything. They just-- they said, we don't know what's going to happen. And we came out very dejected, you know, knowing that's it. And a few minutes later, after we left the building, the whole-- a whole thing of trucks came in with the executioners. So we knew that's it. It's starting. And everybody-- and they start screaming. All the Juden, you know, go all to their own places, where they were yesterday, or you will-- or everybody will be dead. So we're trying to run across to our building. But at the same time, something pulled us back to this-- we were closer to this building. And we were sort of afraid to cross, because they were in our way. And we run into this building, this one we just came out of, where my sister had those influential friends that could have known something. And we stayed in this building. And they-- the moment we walked in, we wanted to get out. Because we thought, everybody in their place. If they want to find us, they'll say, oh, well, you know, they'll shoot us anyway. I don't know. But in this-- this reasoning is-- you act by instinct. You don't act by reason in this particular case, because you just-- your adrenaline works. Something-- you're not yourself. You're just some sort of a machine, by instincts. And we wanted to run out. And then this German with a thing bolted the door and said, nobody comes out. Nobody goes in, nobody goes out! Here, you stay where you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed where we are. They wanted to go into the rooms. This was in the corridor before, the door to the entrance of the building, near the door. Because we had just come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to go to these rooms. And they said, no, there's no room for you. Because the Germans were here yesterday, and they made a selection. And they counted us, and they said if they found one more person than they counted, they whole room will be dead, will be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't want to let us in. So we stayed in that corridor. We thought oh, well, any minute, they'll come. They'll take us. Because they'll count the rooms, and we will be discovered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we looked through the keyholes, and they were emptying the building completely, trucks and trucks. And it was so quiet. Nobody said a word. Everybody just went out on the trucks. And they kept going all the way. And then they went to the second building. They emptied the second building. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the next morning, we found out when they opened the building and said, now you can go out, we went into the building. It was almost empty and the things were gone. And we found out that this building has been already selected the night before, and it was bribed or something. It was-- they had a sort of a-- the Judenrats were sitting there, and the most important, rich, influential people, they did something. They made some-- some deal with the Germans, not to touch this building. And that's how we survived the first-- the first action. The 4,000 people that's taken to the-- to the thing, to the mass graves and shot, machine gunned, just like that. That was the first action. And after that, we went into the ghettos. They took us. They prepared the ghetto. 1,500 people went into the ghetto out of the 6,000. Some of them were living in little-- little villages. And so this-- this was a separate settlement. And those villages later, they took them out to the prison and later shot them. So all together, about 4,000 on this one day, or one and a half days. And that's how, miraculously, the three of us got saved. We went the next day, we tried to get our few possessions that we had, a change of underwear and things like this. We couldn't even find it. It was all mixed up in-- in people. And then we went to the ghetto day after, two days after. And that's when the ghetto was established, in-- in the beginning of January. 19-- in the beginning of-- no, in the middle of September. What, I'm mixing it up. The beginning of December. After the action, a couple of days later, they took us into the ghetto. It was very-- very a part of Nowogrodek. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was-- we were walking in the mud. There was no water. We had to go and get water. There was no heating, there was no-- They piled us in, as many as-- as they could get them into one room, the few houses that they confiscated in this little area. And very hard. That was the beginning of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my two sisters and my-- there was another couple. And there was another couple. It was a small room, a bit smaller than this. Bunks. And we just had-- had enough room to-- 33 centimeters in the width. So we worked it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: A meter for three people. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food? What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, we had to do our own. Ghetto didn't supply any food. We had to look around, sell possession. My sister had clientele outside the ghetto. There was a few Christian people that liked her work, and word got around. And they-- she was influential, because she was a false German. He mother was German, her father was Polish. They were very nice people. And she worked, and she spoke perfect German. So she worked in the Secretariat, in the [GERMAN] Commissariat. Her name was Galia, I guess I remember. Very nice lady. She really-- she really helped us a lot. And she used to take care of my sister to her place. And she would make her dresses and for relatives, and she would bring food.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, I was working in a potato cellar. It was all outside the ghetto. So I was bringing potatoes home. False pockets and things like this, you know? We worked all days in this dark cellar, sorting the rotten ones from the good ones, and you could smuggle out a few potatoes every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a good job. It was considered a very good job. And I worked there, and my-- and my future husband worked there. And so-- but I didn't know him then then. He just was one of the coworkers. And he just lost in the Holocaust, in that first action, he had a wife and three children. They were taken. This particular month, they were taken from him, and he was hiding. But this time, he was living in a village, because his house was burnt up by the bombing. So he had a brother who lived in a-- in a nearby village. So he went to live with him, with his family. And he went to the forest to chop wood or fetch water. I don't know. And this time they came and they took his wife and children. And-- and he came back, they weren't there. So he-- he came to the ghetto, and he got this job in the potato cellars. And that's how we lived. My other sister didn't contribute anything. We had to carry water and go for half a kilometer to-- to carry water. And somebody had to try and get-- make some fire, cook something up. And somebody had to wash. It was very hard to live. It was very primitive and very rough. And people didn't help each other at this particular time, because everybody was helpless themselves, cause they tried to-- so anyway, we needed-- we needed a man badly. My sister lost her husband. We were three helpless women. My-- my sister was-- in respect of food, she used to bring food and sometimes nice things they gave her, you know, where she was making dresses, sewing for them. And then word got around, and the German ladies wanted a dressmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start taking her to the-- to the houses of the German elite, and she make them beautiful clothes. So they somehow gave her food. And so we weren't really badly off, as far as food was concerned. We didn't starve. How long did you live there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there from December, sort of like the first week or second week of December '41 till about eighth of August, seventh, eighth of August '42. That's how many? Nine months? Eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the conditions of the ghetto change within that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conditions of the ghetto changed. Not only-- they installed a tower with the searchlights, you know. They kept searching if we went walking. Because it was dark at night, and there was no electricity. We were trying to, with a bit of oil, a bit of-- because there's no electricity. There's no water. There's no bathing facilities. There's no-- everything was in mud. But people, you know, Jews lived, you know. Somehow we managed to keep clean. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the same time, when my sister was going out and doing all those jobs for the German elite and for the Christians who were connected-- well-connected-- she didn't get paid. She just got food. And I had to go out to the [NON-ENGLISH], and my other sister used to go out and did the other jobs. Variety of jobs, you know, whatever was-- was needed of the day. Once they were building a garage. We had to do the digging and the brick handling, and all this. Very hard physical work. They didn't give us any bread, any money, nothing for us. Just for free. And all of a sudden, they came into an idea that there were other tradesmen, like Jewish tailors or Jewish boot makers and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, the Germans realized, why should they take those people into their homes and back? They can make a profit out of all of this. So put all those people in-- in workrooms, workshops, and they can tell the outside, the Aryan side, the Christian side, here are some tradesmen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they will pay the money, and they will have the profits. They will have-- the Germans will have the profits, and they won't need to take-- so they established some sort of workrooms in a building. This was before it was sort of the [NON-ENGLISH] Commissariat, like a state-- state offices, like the state parliament or something. I don't know. A state something, council. And so they asked my sister to establish this dressmaking. Instead of going to the-- to the clients to work for them, they can come to her, and to take a few other people with her and establish a workroom, so we can serve the Christian community. And they can pay for the services, and the Germans will take the money. And that was a very good idea. And my sister right away, said, I work with my two sisters. I can't work by myself. So they allowed her to take her two sisters. So we ended up working. This was a very big step ahead later, to survival, in the second action. And then they were established. They took in other-- other trades. And they sort of had a workshop from few trades there. They had a shoemaker. They had furriers, who make those fur coats. They had tailors for the suits. They had knitters. They had everything. So they used us up in this way. And for us, it was good, because we were sitting in nice, warm rooms and not working very hard. And we were sort of more assured of our prospects of surviving, because they needed us. They made money out of us. So this was-- later on, this place became too small, and they had-- we returned to the courthouse from which, you know, [LAUGHS] we saved ourselves. And they had a big building converted in there. Because it grew bigger and bigger, so they didn't have enough room in there, in these other buildings. So they built-- took us in there, and we worked there. And in the meantime-- I worked there. In the meantime, after the big action, when everything settled down and people got into the ghettos, they start counting how many people survived the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, we need a list of all the single women. The Gestapo came to the Judenrat. They said, there's too many survived. We need a lot of single women who were not supposed to have survived. Give me a list of all single women. So what-- what do we do? The Judenrat didn't want to give them the list of single women, because they knew what's going to happen to them. So they went quietly into the-- into the huts, into the houses. And they said, people who are single, if there's any single men, please come to the Judenrat and register yourself as husband and wife. Well, I had to look for a man whom I can trust, you know? Because he could-- this wasn't to-- to be for real. It was fictitious. So I was looking around. There was one young man. He said, you know, I might write my name, what he also get married fictitiously. And then came this man, this-- my husband. And much older, 18 years older. The other one looked like a kid, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, if you want-- he knew me, because from work, I had a very sort of good relationship with my workmates. And he had a friend who worked there too, and he sort of saw me. I wasn't one of the-- from the town, because I was a newcomer. So now everybody sort of looked at me as I'm a bit different from them, which I was, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was easy to adjust and to fit in. That's what they liked of me. Started talking, you know, the way-- Yiddish, the ways I spoke. You know? You couldn't recognize it, because I was young, and easy to pick up-- pick up their way of language.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't speaking Yiddish at all when I came to Nowogrodek. Only with my parents, and it was a different Yiddish. And with them, I had to speak the real Lithuanian Yiddish. But I learned it very quickly. And so I adjusted. [INAUDIBLE] didn't stand out from the-- from the local crowd. I adjusted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came over, and he said, you know, if you want to, I can do you this favor. No-- no string attached. He was lonely. He just lost his wife and kids. He was a broken up man. He didn't think of anything. Neither did I. Who-- you know? He wasn't my type at all. We had nothing in common.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in times like this, you just look for something that's real, that's real, that's reliable. So I said, OK, we'll do that. So I went into the-- some men, they wanted money from the-- from women, from single women. They wanted gold watches and things like that, for doing them the favor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said OK. We went to the Judenrat. And we signed the register. We were married, officially, as far as the Jewish authority, which had-- was no authority at all. But among the Jews, this was the authority.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, they came, the Germans. And they didn't find any single women. And my sister got somebody. My other sister got somebody. And you know, it was not-- all of a sudden, you know, it was not binding whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, I go to the-- to get some water. I had to carry two buckets of water that should have last us for the day, for washing, for cooking, for drinking, for anything, two big buckets of water. And in the mud and in the snow, I was carrying two buckets of water. And all of a sudden, I see what is-- this man standing. He said, can I carry your water for you? [LAUGHS]
